# Acknowledgments {.unnumbered}

This book started as a set of teaching notebooks and became something larger because people kept asking the same question: "How should I actually be using this?"

The students who sat through early versions of these ideas deserve credit for their patience and their honesty. When an approach did not work, they said so. When it did, they showed me why — often in ways I had not anticipated. The frameworks in this book were tested in classrooms before they were tested on paper.

Several colleagues provided feedback on drafts, pushed back on arguments that were not yet strong enough, and challenged me to be clearer about what I was actually claiming. The book is sharper for those conversations. You know who you are, and I am grateful.

The open source community behind Quarto, Python, and GitHub made it possible to write, build, and publish this book with tools that are free, transparent, and remarkably good. Self-publishing has never been more accessible, and that is because of their work.

This book was written using the methodology it describes, which means AI was involved at every stage. Claude (Anthropic) served as a conversation partner throughout: drafting, challenging, restructuring, and being told to try again when the output was not good enough. Google Gemini and FLUX Playground generated the comic strip illustrations. The relationship between author and AI in producing this book was exactly what the book argues it should be: a conversation, not a delegation. Every sentence reflects the author's judgement. The AI made the thinking faster. It did not do the thinking.
